text: For nearly a century, the Fischer-Tropsch (FT) process has remained a subject of rigorous scientific debate, particularly regarding the fundamental nature of its active sites and reaction mechanisms on cobalt and iron catalysts. Over the past fifteen years, the field has undergone a paradigm shift driven by a "bottom-up" approach that integrates surface science with molecular modeling. Theoretical frameworks have provided a clearer structural profile of cobalt catalyst particles, while recent surface science experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations have underscored how realistic surface coverages can trigger surface reconstructions and dictate the stability of reaction intermediates. For cobalt-based systems, a consensus on the active sites and mechanism is finally emerging through the convergence of detailed microkinetic simulations and mechanistic experiments. In contrast, iron-based catalysts present a greater challenge; their dynamic phase evolution under reaction conditions complicates the identification of active surface structures. While emerging techniques are beginning to address this combinatorial complexity, the lack of a definitive structural model for iron active sites continues to hinder a unified mechanistic understanding. Finally, exploring the direct hydrogenation of CO₂ into long-chain hydrocarbons offers a promising and sustainable frontier for the future of Fischer-Tropsch synthesis.
